coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of a carbon dots modified g-C3N4/SnO2 Z-scheme photocatalyst with superior photocatalytic activity for PPCPs degradation under visible light irradiation

coredata.dc:description: As an emerging carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have superior prospects for applications in the area of photocatalysis due to their unique optical and electronic properties. In this study, a novel CDs modified g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst (CDs/g-C3N4/ SnO2) was successfully synthesized by the thermal polymerization. Under visible light irradiation, the resulting CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of indomethacin (IDM). It was demonstrated that a 0.5 % loading content of CDs led to the highest IDM degradation rate, which was 5.62 times higher than that of pristine g-C3N4. This improved photocatalytic activity might have been attributed to the unique up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) properties and efficient charge separation capacities of the CDs. Moreover, the combination of g-C3N4 with SnO2 improved the separation of photoinduced carriers and augmented the specific surface area. Reactive species (RSs) scavenging experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) revealed that superoxide radical anions (O2 ·−) and photogenerated holes (h+) played critical roles during the photocatalytic process. The results of the detection of H2O2 and ESR confirmed that CDs/g-C3N4/ SnO2 was a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst. Further, HRAM LC–MS/MS was employed to identify the byproducts of IDM, and the major IDM degradation pathways of the CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst were proposed. This study provides new ideas for the design of novel CDs modified photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As an emerging carbon nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have superior prospects for applications in the area of photocatalysis due to their unique optical and electronic properties. In this study, a novel CDs modified g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst (CDs/g-C3N4/ SnO2) was successfully synthesized by the thermal polymerization. Under visible light irradiation, the resulting CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of indomethacin (IDM). It was demonstrated that a 0.5 % loading content of CDs led to the highest IDM degradation rate, which was 5.62 times higher than that of pristine g-C3N4. This improved photocatalytic activity might have been attributed to the unique up-conversion photoluminescence (PL) properties and efficient charge separation capacities of the CDs. Moreover, the combination of g-C3N4 with SnO2 improved the separation of photoinduced carriers and augmented the specific surface area. Reactive species (RSs) scavenging experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) revealed that superoxide radical anions (O2 ·−) and photogenerated holes (h+) played critical roles during the photocatalytic process. The results of the detection of H2O2 and ESR confirmed that CDs/g-C3N4/ SnO2 was a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst. Further, HRAM LC–MS/MS was employed to identify the byproducts of IDM, and the major IDM degradation pathways of the CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst were proposed. This study provides new ideas for the design of novel CDs modified photocatalysts for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) have been of increasing serious scientific and public concern as newly recognized environmental pollutants. This is due to their frequent occurrence, persistent presence in the environment, and health risks to aquatic organisms and humans (Evgenidou et al., 2015; Wang and Wang, 2016). PPCPs are typically found in water and wastewater in concentrations of parts-per-trillion (ng/L) to parts-per-billion (μg/L) (Liu and Wong, 2013); however, studies have revealed that the efficiency of PPCPs removal through conventional wastewater treatment processes is low (Yang et al., 2017a). Therefore, the treatments of PPCPs in water have been investigated in various systems, such as ozone systems, ultraviolet systems, Fenton oxidation, and photocatalytic oxidation (Yuan et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2016; Wang and Wang, 2017). Notably, eco-friendly photocatalytic materials have the capacity to redox pollutants under light irradiation without the requirement of electrical energy or external chemical reagents (Di Paola et al., 2012; Bai et al., 2019), which makes them an ideal technique for the treatment of PPCPs in water (Zhao et al., 2018; Sharma et al., 2019).

As a fascinating conjugated polymer, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with a narrow band gap (∼2.7 eV) has been the focus of intense interest in the field of materials as a visible light-driven photocatalyst (Zhu et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2019a). However, pure g-C3N4 exhibits limited photocatalytic activity due to low specific surface area, insufficient light utilization, and the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers (Mamba and Mishra, 2016a). To overcome the limitations of g-C3N4, several strategies have been devised, including elemental doping, morphological control, and formation of heterojunctions with other nanometric semiconductors (Fang et al., 2016; Lu et al., 2019; Fu et al., 2018). In particular, g-C3N4-based heterostructures generally enhance charge separation and transfer, thus improving photocatalytic performance (Mamba and Mishra, 2016b). Therefore, g-C3N4-based heterostructures such as g-C3N4/BiVO4 (Li et al., 2014), Co3O4/g-C3N4 (Suyana et al., 2017), MoS2/g-C3N4 (Liu et al., 2018a), and Ag3PO4/g-C3N4 (Mei et al., 2018) have been successfully investigated and applied in environmental and energy fields.

Recently, the n-type semiconductor, tin dioxide (SnO2), has been extensively used in the area of photocatalysts (Wang et al., 2013). This is because SnO2 is a good electron acceptor that possesses an extensive specific surface area, which may suppress the recombination of photoinduced carriers and provide additional reaction sites, thus improving photocatalytic performance (Zang et al., 2014; Uddin et al., 2012). Previous studies have reported that photocatalysts formed through the combination of SnO2 with g-C3N4 can effectively degrade contaminants in water under visible light (Praus et al., 2018; Shen et al., 2017). However, despite intensive efforts, several challenges and problems remain for g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalysts. These include that SnO2 has an unusual wide band gap (3.5∼3.7 eV); thus, it can only activated by ultraviolet light (Yin et al., 2014). This results in a limited capacity of the g-C3N4/SnO2 composite to utilize visible light. Therefore, to obtain higher photocatalytic activity, it is necessary to improve the visible light adsorption performance of this composite.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new class of carbon nanomaterial, are comprised of hybrid sp2/sp3 carbon atoms with various surface functional groups. They have received broad interest because of their unique attributes that encompass high aqueous solubility, up-conversion PL behavior, and exceptional photoelectron transfer (Lim et al., 2015; ReddyPrasad and Naidoo, 2015; Zheng et al., 2015). Furthermore, the absorption range of CDs overlaps with most of the solar spectrum, which is beneficial for improving the light absorption capacities of CDs modified composites (Fernando et al., 2015; Luo et al., 2013). Hence, CDs have been successfully combined with many semiconductors (e.g., CDs/TiO2 (Chen et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2017), CDs/BiPO4 (Zhang et al., 2018), CDs/g-C3N4 (Liu et al., 2019)) toward improving their photocatalytic activities through augmenting the utilization of light and inhibiting photogenerated electron/hole recombination. Considering the limitations of the g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst and the outstanding attributes of CDs, it may be considered that coupling CDs with g-C3N4/SnO2 will form a stable and highly efficient photocatalytic nanocomposite. To the best of our knowledge, CDs modified g-C3N4/SnO2 for the degradation of PPCPs has not been reported to date.

Herein, a high-efficiency CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalyst was prepared by facile thermal polymerization, which demonstrated excellent photocatalytic performance for the degradation of indomethacin (IDM) (a typical PPCPs (Temussi et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2017a)) under visible light irradiation, in contrast to pristine g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/SnO2. This research included the preparation and characterization (e.g., microstructure, morphology, optical, and electrochemical properties) of the as- synthesized samples. Furthermore, the effects of different CDs content, catalyst loading, light source irradiation, and pH conditions on the photocatalytic performance of the CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 were also investigated. The active species contributing to the CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 were detected through ESR technology and RSs quenching experiments. Subsequently, potential photocatalytic transformation pathways and reaction mechanisms for IDM degradation were proposed.

The IDM (98 % purity) was obtained from TCI Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Guanidinium hydrochloride and tin chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4·5H2O) were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytical grade citric acid and urea were purchased from Titan Scientific Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HPLC-grade methanol was obtained from CNW Technologies GmbH (Shanghai, China). Deionized (DI) water (= 18.25 MΩ.cm resistivity) from a Milli-Q apparatus (Karlsruhe, Germany) was used throughout this research.

The g-C3N4 (hereinafter referred to as CN) powder was prepared using a thermal condensation method (Huang et al., 2019). In detail, 3.0 g guanidinium hydrochloride powder was added into an alumina crucible, and then heated in a muffle furnace at 550 °C for 4 h with a ramp rate of 2 °C min−1. Once cooled to room temperature, the obtained bulk CN was ground into powder.

The SnO2 powder was prepared via a hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2.1 g SnCl4·5H2O was dissolved in 70 mL of DI water and the pH was adjusted to 8 with NaOH solution. Following 1 h of ultrasonic agitation, the liquor was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the white product was collected via centrifugation, and rinsed several times with ethanol and DI water, and finally dried at 60 °C for 24 h.

The CN/SnO2 composite was synthesized through a simple calcination technique (Zang et al., 2014). Different quantities of CN and SnO2 were dispersed in 20 mL ethanol, after which the mixture was heated in water bath under magnetic stirring at 60 °C to remove the ethanol. Finally, the obtained solid was transferred in a muffle furnace and calcined at 400 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 2 °C min−1. The as-synthesized nanocomposites were correspondingly designated as CN/SnO2-x (CSnx), where x represented the weight ratios of SnO2 to CN. As shown in (Fig. S6), when the mass ratio was 20 %, CSn20 exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance.

The CDs were obtained by a hydrothermal method (Zhang et al., 2018). Typically, 3.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of urea were dispersed in 10 mL of DI water. Subsequently, the mixed solution was added into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 5 h. Once the obtained black solution was cooled to room temperature, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 30 min to separate any large particles. Subsequent to collection, the resulting supernatant was reconstituted to a solution of 20 g/L with DI water.

The preparation of the CDs/CN/SnO2 hybrids was as follows: Different quantities of the CSn20 and CDs were fully dispersed in 20 mL of ethanol. Subsequently, the mixture was evaporated at 60 °C under magnetic stirring to completely remove the ethanol. The obtained dry-powder was heated in a muffle furnace at 300 °C for 3 h with a ramp rate of 5 °C min−1. Similarly, the as-synthesized composites were correspondingly denoted as y-CDs/CSn20 (yCCSn20) in which y represented the nominal weight ratios of the CDs to CSn20. For comparison, the CDs/CN (CCN) and CDs/SnO2 (CSnO2) were prepared by the same method described above.

The morphologies of the as-prepared photocatalysts were studied using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100HR), which was equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The crystal structure of the products was characterized via X-ray diffraction (XRD, BrukerAXS, D8Advance) with Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi) equipped with Mg-Kα radiation and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, Thermofisher Nicolet 6700 spectrophotometer) were employed to test the chemical components and bonding characteristics of the samples. The UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples were investigated through a UV–vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-3600Plus, SHIMADZU, Japan) with the BaSO4 as a reflectance standard. The TOC removal ratio of the as-prepared photocatalysts was detected using a TOC-VCPH analyzer (SHIMADZU, Japan). The PL spectra were recorded on a transient steady-state fluorescence spectrometer. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area were characterized through N2 adsorption-desorption at −196 °C using an Autosorb-IQ instrument.

The performance of the as-prepared photocatalysts was evaluated via the photocatalytic degradation of IDM. A 420 nm cut-off filter was placed between the light source (350 W xenon lamp) and reactor (Xujiang Electromechanical Plant, China) to obtain the visible light. To examine the activities of the photocatalyst under different light sources, four 9 W LED lamps (Shenzhen Lamplic Technology Co. LTD., China) were utilized to generate UV light (390 nm), blue light (450 nm), green light (520 nm), and red light (660 nm). A circulating water bath was employed to maintain the reaction temperature at 25.0 ± 2 °C. Typically, 10 mg of catalysts were dispersed in 50 mL IDM aqueous solution (10 mg L−1), the mixture was stirred in dark for 40 min to attain the IDM adsorption equilibrium. Under light irradiation, 1.0 mL samples were extracted every 20 min, and then filtered through 0.45 μm syringe filters to remove the catalysts. All experiments were performed in triplicate and the error bars represent standard deviations (with 95 % confidence intervals). The concentration of IDM was detected via high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, SHIMADZU LC16, Japan). The detailed information of the analytical methods is displayed in the Text S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of RSs and identification of degradation intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: A scavenger-quenching experiment was used to identify the RSs of the IDM photodegradation process. In a typical trial, scavengers including isopropanol (IPA, 10 mM), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4, 10 mM), 4-hydroxy-2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidinyloxy (TEMPO, 1.0 mM) were employed to quench hydroxyl radicals (·OH), photogenerated holes (h+), and superoxide iron radicals (O2 ·−), respectively. Moreover, sodium azide (NaN3, 75 mM) was employed as a ·OH and singlet oxygen (1O2) scavenger. The ESR signals of RSs were directly detected on a Bruker JES FA200 spectrometer. To further elucidate the photocatalytic mechanisms of IDM degradation, photodegradation intermediates were identified by mixing samples collected at 20, 40, and 80 min of irradiation for HRAM LC–MS/MS analysis. The details of HRAM LC–MS/MS system are shown in Text S2.

An electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E, China) was employed to measure the transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS), and Mott-Schottky plots. All electrochemical measurements were performed using a three-electrode system, with a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 10 mg of the sample was added in a mixture containing 20 μL Nafion and 2 mL ethanol. Following 30 min of sonication, the suspension was applied dropwise onto the surface of the ITO glass. After drying at 60 °C, the prepared working electrode was placed in a 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte solution, and irradiated with a 450 nm LED lamp to record the photocurrent.

The TEM image of Fig. 1 a depicts the spherical morphology of the CDs, with an average diameter of ∼3 nm (Fig. 1a inset). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1b) and fast Fourier transform (FFT) pattern (Fig. 1c) clearly display the hexagonal crystalline structure of the CDs, with an interplanar spacing of 0.203 nm, which corresponded well to the (101) lattice planes of graphite carbon (Liu et al., 2015). Fig. 1d shows TEM images of the 0.5CCSn20 composite with spherical SnO2 particles dispersed on the surface of the layered CN. The elemental mapping images shown in Fig. 1e–h further confirmed that C, N, Sn, and O were dispersed within the 0.5CCSn20 matrix. The HRTEM image of Fig. 1i demonstrated that CDs were also dispersed on the CN surface. Moreover, the HRTEM image (Fig. 1j) indicated that the SnO2 exhibited lattice fringes with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, which could be assigned to the (110) planes of SnO2 (Liu et al., 2018b).

The XRD patterns of the CN, SnO2, CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 are presented in Fig. 2 a. For pure CN, there were two distinct characteristic peaks at two-theta of 12.9° and 27.4°, which were indexed to the in-planar structural periodic array of tri-s-triazine units ((100) planes) and the interlayer stacking structure of conjugated aromatic systems ((002) planes) (JCPDS Card No. 87- 1526) (Shi et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2017b). In addition, the XRD pattern of SnO2 revealed a group of diffraction peaks located at 26.6°, 33.9°, 37.9°, 51.8°, 54.7°, 61.8°, 64.7°, and 65.9°, which corresponded to the (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (310), (112) and (301) crystal planes of SnO2 (JCPDS Card No. 41-1445), respectively(Mao et al., 2014). Comparatively, the XRD patterns of the CSn20 consisted of two sets of diffraction data for both the CN and SnO2, which verified that the SnO2 was successfully anchored onto the CN (Zang et al., 2014). The XRD patterns of the 0.5CCSn20 were similar to that of the CSn20, which was potentially due the low carbon quantum dots doping content of the composite material (Xie et al., 2018). Furthermore, no impurity peaks of other characteristics were found during the synthesis, which indicated that a high purity crystalline state was maintained during the synthesis process.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the CN, CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 2b. It can be seen that the physical adsorption isotherms of CN, CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 exhibited type IV with distinct H3 hysteresis loops in the P/P0 range of from 0.65 to 1.0, which indicated that they existed as mesoporous structures (Tian et al., 2017). The specific surface area of the 0.5CCSn20 was 53.717 m²/g, which was significantly larger than that of the CN (38.702 m²/g). This might have been attributed to the combination of the CN and SnO2, which had a large specific surface area (94.204 m²/g) (Fig. S1) (Chen et al., 2015). Moreover, the specific surface area of the 0.5CCSn20 was slightly lower than that of the CSn20 (55.835 m²/g), which might have been caused by the loaded CDs occupying a proportion of the spaces within the CSn20 (Wang et al., 2018).

As illustrated in Fig. 3 , the surface composition and chemical state of the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). For comparison, the XPS spectra of the CN and SnO2 were also studied, as shown in Fig. S2. The XPS survey spectra of CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 (Fig. 3a) verified the existence of C, N, O, and Sn on their surfaces. In particular, the C content of 0.5CCSn20 was 70.64 %, which slightly higher than that of CSn20 (70.03 %) due to the doping of CDs (Zhang et al., 2019b). The high-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s, N1s, O1s, and Sn3d of the photocatalysts are illustrated in Fig. 3b–e. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the high-resolution C1s spectrum was fitted into three peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.2 eV, which were assigned to C–C, C–O, and N = C–N, respectively (Gao et al., 2018). The C–C intensity (64.61 %) of the 0.5CCSn20 was higher than that of the CSn20 (61.24 %), which may have been attributed to the successful incorporation of CDs. The N1s XPS spectra peaks (Fig. 3c) of the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20, located at 398.5, 399.3, 401.0, and 404.2 eV were ascribed to C = N–C, N–(C)3, N–H, and graphitic species, respectively (Guo et al., 2016). The O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3d) exhibited three main peaks at 530.2, 531.7, and 533.1 eV, which were assigned to lattice oxygen in SnO2 (Kou et al., 2018), C–OH, and C = O, respectively (Xie et al., 2018). Fig. 3e depicts the high-resolution XPS spectra of Sn3d in the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 samples. For pure SnO2, there were two typical peaks at 486.8 and 495.3 eV (Fig. S2d), which corresponded to Sn3d5/2 and Sn3d3/2 for the Sn4+ in SnO2, respectively (Dursun et al., 2018). However, for the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20, the binding energies of Sn3d5/2 and Sn3d3/2 characteristic peaks shifted slightly to 486.4 and 494.8 eV, respectively (Fig. S2e), which have been attributed to the interactions between the SnO2 and CN (Chen et al., 2015).

The bonding characteristics of CN, SnO2, CSn20, and 0.5CCSn20 were detected through the FTIR spectra (Fig. 3f). As for CN, a sharp absorption band at 808 cm−1 was attributed to the breathing vibrations of triazine units, whereas the wider band at ∼3430 cm−1 corresponded to the C–OH, H2O, and N–H stretching vibrations (Ong et al., 2015; Zhou et al., 2015). The strong vibration peaks located at 1200−1650 cm−1 were ascribed to the typical C–N and C = N stretching vibrations of the CN heterocycles (Pan et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2018). As for the SnO2, the characteristic peaks at ∼564 cm−1 and 659 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of Sn-O bonds (Liu et al., 2018b). Moreover, the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 showed similar spectra to the CN; in particular, the stretching vibration of Sn–O at 659 cm−1 in the spectra verified the successful synthesis of the CSn20 composite, which was consistent with the XRD results. In addition, there was no significant difference in the infrared spectra of the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20, which indicated that the loading of CDs did not alter the chemical structure of the CSn20 (Wang et al., 2018).

The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) was employed to investigate the optical properties of the CN, SnO2, CSn20, and 0.5CCSn20. As depicted in Fig. 4 a, pristine CN revealed a typical absorption edge at ∼450 nm, while the SnO2 merely responded to ultraviolet light. The absorption edges of the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 were approximately at 418 and 427 nm. Compared with CN, the CSn20 exhibited a remarkable blue-shift, which might have been due to the interactions between the CN and SnO2 (Chen et al., 2015). However, the 0.5CCSn20 showed a slight red-shift in contrast to the CSn20, which might have been attributed to the loading of CDs (Wang et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2016). Therefore, the optical properties of CDs were further explored by photoluminescence spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S3, when excited by longer wavelengths (550−900 nm), the CDs emitted shorter (350−600 nm) wavelengths and up-converted photoluminescence, which verified their up-conversion performance. Meanwhile, it could be seen that the light absorption capacity of 0.5CCSn20 was increased compared with the CSn20. This might be ascribed to the intimate contact between the CDs and CSn20, which served as photosensitizers on the surface of the CSn20 (Chen et al., 2018). Further, the band gap energy (Eg) of the CN and SnO2 can be calculated according to the Kubelka-Munk formula: α h v = A
( h v - E g )
n / 2
. The various parameters of the formula, α , h , v , A, and Eg are the absorption coefficient, Planck’s constant, light frequency, a constant, and band gap energy, respectively. Based on the optical transition characteristics of semiconductors, the n values of the CN and SnO2 are 4 and 1, respectively (Chen et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 4b, the Eg of the CN and SnO2 were estimated to be 2.68 and 3.66 eV, respectively.

To further investigate the relative band positions of the CN and SnO2, Mott-Schottky plots and valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) were performed (Gong et al., 2018). As depicted in Fig. S4a-b, the flat-band potentials of the CN and SnO2 obtained from the Mott-Schottky plot were -1.18 and -0.56 eV versus saturated calomel electrode (SCE), respectively, which were calculated to be -0.60 and 0.02 eV versus the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) (Yang et al., 2018; Ma et al., 2019). As is well known, the flat-band potential of n-type semiconductor equals the Fermi level (Li et al., 2016). Therefore, the Fermi levels of CN and SnO2 were estimated to be -0.60 and 0.02 eV, respectively. The VB-XPS results (Fig. S4c–d) revealed the energy gap between the Fermi level and the valence band (VB). Consequently, the VBs of the CN and SnO2 were calculated to be 1.75 and 3.48 eV. Subsequently, the conduction band (CB) positions of the CN and SnO2 were determined to be -0.93 and -0.18 eV, according to the formula: ECB = EVB - Eg. The band structures of the CN and SnO2 are shown in Fig. S5.

The steady-state PL spectra of the CN, CSn20, and 0.5CCSn20 were shown in Fig. 5 a to investigate their photogenerated electron/hole pairs separation efficiencies. In general, a lower PL intensity represented a higher separation efficiency rate of photogenerated carriers (Yang et al., 2013). Compared with the CN, the PL intensity of the CSn20 decreased significantly, which implied that the combination with SnO2 could inhibit the recombination rate of the photogenerated electron/hole pairs. Among these photocatalysts, the 0.5CCSn20 had the lowest PL intensity, which signified that the loading of CDs successfully improved the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers (Zhang et al., 2018).

The modulated electronic structures of the CN, CSn20, and 0.5CCSn20 were investigated through room temperature electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy. As seen in Fig. 5b, all of the as-prepared samples exhibited a single Lorentzian line with a g value of 2.002, which corresponded to unpaired electrons on the carbon atoms of the aromatic rings within π-bonded nanoclusters (Zhang et al., 2012). Moreover, the EPR signal intensity of the CSn20 was stronger than that of the CN, which further strengthened following the introduction of CDs. This result might have been due to the formation of an improved electronic structure, with an extended delocalization of the π-conjugated system following the loading of CDs (Wang et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2017b). The production of photogenerated charge carriers could be efficiently enhanced through this extended π-conjugated system.

To further probe the photoinduced behaviors of charge transfer and separation, transient photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed. As can be seen in Fig. 5c, the photocurrent intensity of the 0.5CCSn20 composite was much higher than that of the CN and CSn20, which translated to the higher separation of photogenerated charge carriers (Wang et al., 2018). Notably, compared with the CN, the photocurrent of CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 declined steadily when the light was turned off, possibly because the electrons were transferred through the heterostructure and the CDs, and then gradually migrated to the cathode. The results demonstrated that the structure of the heterojunction and the introduction of the CDs inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs (Xu et al., 2019). The EIS measurements shown in Fig. 5d revealed that the Nyquist plots of the 0.5CCSn20 composite possessed a smaller arc radius, in contrast to the CN and CSn20, which suggested a lower interfacial charge transfer resistance and higher charge transfer efficiency (Wang et al., 2019).

The results conveyed above confirmed that the CN and SnO2 were well blended via thermal polymerization; thus, the CDs were successfully loaded onto the surface of this composite. This combination with SnO2 increased the specific surface area and remarkably inhibited the recombination of photoinduced electron/hole pairs, while the introduction of CDs enhanced the capacity for light absorption and significantly improved the electron transfer rate.

The performance of the as-prepared photocatalysts was evaluated through the degradation of IDM under visible light irradiation (λ ≥ 420 nm). As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the removal of IDM was negligible under visible light irradiation for 80 min, which demonstrated that the IDM was photochemically stable. Following 40 min of reaction in the dark, complete adsorption-desorption equilibrium was attained between the photocatalysts and the IDM solution. Particularly, although the SnO2 could not be excited by visible light, it could absorb 27 % of the IDM due to its extensive specific surface area. Following the loading of CDs, the CSnO2 could slightly photodegrade the IDM under visible light irradiation. Obviously, after 80 min of visible light irradiation, the photocatalytic degradation of IDM by CSn20 was 70.3 %, which was significantly higher than that of the CN (32.0 %). Nearly 90.8 % of the IDM was removed over the same timeline following the introduction of the CDs. According to the pseudo first-order rate constants (Fig. 6b), the degradation rate constants of the CN, CCN, CSn20, and 0.5CCSn20 were 0.0042, 0.0104, 0.0126, and 0.0236 min−1, respectively. The degradation rate constant of the 0.5CCSn20 was 5.62 and 1.87 times higher than that of the CN and CSn20, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 6c, pure CDs did not have ability to photodegrade IDM under visible light irradiation. When the loading of CDs was increased from 0 to 0.5 wt %, the kinetic rate constant gradually increased from 0.0126 to 0.0236 min−1. However, excessive CDs (> 0.5 wt %) loads resulted in decreased photocatalytic performance. This suppression might have been attributed to the CDs optical shielding effect, which occurred due to the competition with CSn20 for the absorption of photons (Zhang et al., 2018, 2019b).

Fig. 6d shows the effects of different 0.5CCSn20 concentrations on the photocatalytic degradation of IDM under visible light irradiation. As the quantity of added 0.5CCSn20 increased from 0.1 to 0.6 g/L, the amount of IDM adsorbed by the 0.5CCSn20 in the dark reaction increased from 14.9 % to 43.3 %, whereas the photodegradation rate constants rose from 0.0081 to 0.0712 min−1. These results demonstrated that as the 0.5CCSn20 content increased, the contact area between the photocatalyst and IDM expanded, which boosted the number of active sites and enhanced the degradation rate.

To further assess the up-conversion functions and transfer properties of the CDs, the photocatalytic activities of the CSn20 and 0.5CCSn20 in the degradation of IDM were compared under different types of light irradiation. As depicted in Fig. 6e, under visible light, UV light, blue light, green light, and red light, the photocatalytic performance of the 0.5CCSn20 was 1.87, 1.12, 1.68, 6.13, and infinity times higher than that of CSn20, respectively. These results demonstrated the up-conversion abilities of the CDs in the 0.5CCSn20. In particular, the SnO2 was excited under ultraviolet light, leading to the production of more reactive species, which greatly enhanced the degradation rate of IDM. Moreover, the CDs exhibited a higher up-conversion fluorescence response under longer wavelength light irradiation, which has been confirmed by fluorescence spectrum (Fig. S3) (Han et al., 2014).

The effects of different initial pH were also studied for the degradation of IDM. As shown in Fig. 6f, the observed rate constants for IDM degradation decreased significantly, from 0.0366 min−1 at pH 4.0 to 0.0015 min−1 at pH 10. The zero potential of 0.5CCSn20 was ∼3.64 (Fig. S7), and the dissociation constant (pKa) of the IDM was 4.5 (Li et al., 2018), which revealed that the photocatalyst surface remained negative at pH > 3.64, while the IDM was predominantly cationic at pH < 4.5. When the pH of the solution was less than 4.5, an electrostatic attraction might occur between the 0.5CCSn20 and the protonated IDM, which accelerated the photolytic activity of the 0.5CCSn20 system. On the contrary, when the pH exceeded 4.5, the surface of 0.5CCSn20 photocatalyst remained negative and the IDM was presented mainly as anionic species, which might initiate strong electrostatic repulsion between them, resulting in weak photocatalytic activity.

To evaluate the photocatalytic stability of the 0.5CCSn20 composite, recycling experiments were conducted for the photodegradation of IDM under identical conditions (Fig. 7 a). Following four cycles, the IDM removal rate remained at ∼80.1 %, which was just slightly lower than that of the first round. Further, there were no significant changes in the morphological structure (Fig. S8) and characteristic stretching (Fig. 7b) even after four cycles, which indicated the 0.5CCSn20 composite exhibited high stability and reusability.

To study the photocatalytic mechanisms of the 0.5CCSn20 composite, the ESR technology was performed using radical trapping agents (Chen et al., 2016). The 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyridine N-oxide (DMPO)-·OH, DMPO-O2 ·− and 4-oxo-2,2,6,6- tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP)-1O2 signals of the catalysts were detected under dark conditions, at 5 and 10 min of light irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. 8 a–c, no ESR signals could be observed under dark conditions, but the 0.5CCSn20 showed strong DMPO-·OH, DMPO-O2 ·−, and TEMP-1O2 signals under visible light irradiation, which indicated the generation of ·OH, O2 ·−, and 1O2 during the photocatalytic degradation. Furthermore, the CSn20 exhibited stronger ESR signals than did the CN due to its combination with SnO2. However, all ESR signals of the 0.5CCSn20 composite were much stronger than those of the CN and CSn20, which confirmed that the introduction of CDs generated additional ·OH, O2 ·−, and 1O2, thereby improving photocatalytic performance.

To further determine the contributions of the different reactive species in the IDM degradation process for the 0.5CCSn20 composite, a quenching experiment was conducted using IPA, NaN3, Na2C2O4, and TEMPO as ·OH, 1O2, h+, and O2 ·− scavengers, respectively (Huang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2015). As illustrated in Fig. 8d, the observed rate constants for IDM degradation of the blank control and solutions containing IPA, NaN3, Na2C2O4, and TEMPO were 0.0233, 0.0199, 0.0053, 0.0068, and 0.0012 min−1, respectively. Additionally, the contribution rates for IDM degradation under the same conditions were calculated to be 14.6 %, 62.7 %, 70.8 %, and 94.8 %, respectively. Notably, due to the complex free radical chemistry involved in the photocatalytic mechanism, the total contribution rate exceeded 100 % (Chen et al., 2019). These results revealed that h+ and O2 ·− played critical roles in the degradation of IDM.

Additionally, the photocatalytic generation of H2O2 was detected to further study the mechanism of ·OH formation. The details of the analytical methods are displayed in the Text S3. The result showed that the CN system generated more H2O2 than that of the 0.5CCSn20 (Fig. S9). For the CN system, the H2O2 was generated through the reduction of O2 (O2 +2H+ + 2e− → H2O2), which was then further reduced by photogenerated electrons to produce ·OH (Zhang et al., 2019c). Compared with the CN system, the 0.5CCSn20 system generated less H2O2, but the ESR results revealed that it produced more
OH. Therefore, these results indicated that the production of
OH in the 0.5CCSn20 system was not mainly provided by reducing H2O2, but through the oxidation reaction of photogenerated holes with water (Huang et al., 2020).

Based on the above results and discussions, a direct Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism for the 0.5CCSn20 was proposed, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Due to the up-converted PL properties of the CDs, a portion of visible light and long-wavelength infrared light were converted into short-wavelength light for 0.5CCSn20 adsorption (Wang et al., 2019). Both the CN and SnO2 were excited to generate photogenerated electrons and holes (Eq. (1)). Subsequently, the electrons on the CB of SnO2 were transferred to the VB of CN, which effectively inhibited the recombination of photoinduced electron/hole pairs and maintained the redox ability of single photocatalyst. Meanwhile, additional electrons on the CB of CN were transferred to CDs due to their excellent charge transfer properties, further suppressing the recombination of photogenerated carriers. Considering that the CB potential of CN (-0.93 eV) was higher than the standard reduction potential of O2/O2 ·− (-0.33 eV) (Chen et al., 2017), the electrons on the CB of CN would reduce O2 to generate O2 ·− (Eq. (2)). In addition, the VB potential of the SnO2 (+3.48 eV) was much lower than the standard oxidation potential of ·OH/OH− (+1.99 eV) and ·OH/H2O (+2.68 eV) (Tang et al., 2020), which indicated that the photoinduced holes could directly oxidize OH− and H2O to generate ·OH (Eqs. (3)–(4)). Moreover, the O2 ·− would react with h+ to form 1O2 (Eq. (5)), while h+ would also directly oxidize and degrade the IDM. Finally, RSs (·OH, 1O2, h+, and O2 ·−) effectively attacked IDM, causing decomposition and mineralization.

The total organic carbon (TOC) removal rate was measured to assess the mineralization of IDM during the photocatalytic degradation (Fig. 9 ). As can be seen, approximately 90.8 % of IDM was degraded after 80 min of treatment, while only 29.4 % of the TOC was eliminated, which indicated that the IDM might be decomposed to other relatively stable substances. However, 41.2 % of the TOC was removed after 160 min of visible light irradiation, signifying that the IDM might be completely mineralized into CO2 and H2O following a sufficiently long treatment time (Wang et al., 2019).

To explore the photocatalytic pathways associated with IDM degradation, potential intermediates were identified by HRAM LC/MS/MS system, which confirmed eight major transformation products. The supposed structures and the secondary ion mass spectra are shown in Fig. S10, subsequently, four major pathways were determined as shown in Scheme 2 . The pathwayⅠbegan with the cleavage of a CN bond. According to previous studies (Wang et al., 2017), the C13 with lower electron cloud density on the IDM was vulnerable to nucleophilic O2 ·− attack through a nucleophilic reaction, resulting in a peroxide production, which subsequently lead to the cleavage of CN bond and the formation of P1 (m/z 156) and P2 (m/z 219). The P2 was further decarboxylated and oxidized by ·OH to form P3 (m/z 209). In terms of pathway Ⅱ, when C10 was attacked by O2 ·−, the C9 formed a methyl radical due to intermediate cleavage. Subsequent to decarboxylation, the C9 methyl radical underwent hydrogen transfer to generate P5 (m/z 313). On one hand, P5 was further oxidized by RSs to form P4 (m/z 189) (Temussi et al., 2011). On the other hand, P5 might be further attacked by h+ or O2 ·− to cause a double bond break between C8 and C11 to form P6 (m/z 304). Pathway Ⅲ was initiated by a RSs attack on the 8C = 11C double bond and 5 C–8 C single bond, and successively hydroxyl addition to generate P6 (Wang et al., 2019). For pathway Ⅳ, the electrophilic ·OH attacked the 8C = 11C double bond through an addition reaction to generate P7 (m/z 391), which was further decarboxylated and demethylated to form P8 (m/z 333). Finally, a portion of these intermediates underwent further ring opening and mineralization to CO2 and H2O.

In summary, we successfully synthesized a high-efficiency CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 Z-scheme photocatalyst through thermal polymerization. Compared with pristine g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/SnO2, the photocatalytic performance of the CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 was significantly higher toward the degradation of IDM under visible light irradiation. The formation of direct Z-scheme heterojunction greatly inhibited the recombination of photogenerated electron/hole pairs and optimized the redox potential of this photocatalytic system. It was demonstrated that the unique up-conversion PL properties and effective charge separation abilities of the CDs greatly augmented the photocatalytic activity. The ESR technique and RSs quenching experiments verified that h+ and O2 ·− played critical roles during the CDs/g-C3N4/SnO2 photocatalytic process. Moreover, the degradation pathways of IDM were proposed through the identification of transformation products. We are hopeful that our work will inspire new strategies for the application of photocatalysts in the treatment of PPCPs in water.
